news_summary: Today's space news highlights NASA's groundbreaking discoveries and advancements, including unexpected observations of a giant tsunami, progress in lunar and Mars exploration through Artemis II, intriguing evidence of ancient complex carbon-based life on Mars, and innovative plans for sustainable moon agriculture.
news_summary_fr: L'actualité spatiale d'aujourd'hui met en avant les découvertes et avancées révolutionnaires de la NASA, notamment des observations inattendues d'un tsunami géant, les progrès réalisés dans l'exploration de la Lune et de Mars grâce à la mission Artemis II, des indices intrigants de l'existence d'une forme de vie ancienne, complexe et à base de carbone sur Mars, ainsi que des projets innovants en matière d'agriculture lunaire durable.
news_summary_es: Las noticias espaciales de hoy destacan los descubrimientos y avances revolucionarios de la NASA, entre los que se incluyen observaciones inesperadas de un tsunami gigante, los avances en la exploración lunar y marciana a través de la misión Artemis II, pruebas intrigantes de la existencia de vida antigua y compleja basada en el carbono en Marte, y planes innovadores para una agricultura lunar sostenible.

articles.title: A NASA satellite caught a giant tsunami doing something no one expected
articles.summary: A Pacific-wide tsunami triggered by a magnitude 8.8 Kamchatka earthquake gave scientists their first detailed satellite view of a major tsunami in motion. The observations revealed unexpected wave behavior and helped uncover a larger earthquake rupture than earlier models predicted.
articles.content:
When a massive earthquake struck off Russia's Kamchatka Peninsula in late July, it unleashed a tsunami that raced across the Pacific Ocean. As the giant waves spread outward, an advanced NASA satellite happened to be in a perfect position to observe the event in unprecedented detail.

The satellite, called Surface Water Ocean Topography (SWOT), recorded the first high resolution, wide area view of a major tsunami generated by a subduction zone earthquake, according to research published in The Seismic Record.

What scientists found was unexpected. Rather than moving across the ocean as a relatively simple wave, the tsunami displayed a far more complicated pattern, with waves spreading, scattering, and interacting across vast stretches of the Pacific. The discovery could help researchers improve tsunami forecasting and better understand potential risks to coastal communities.

A Rare View of a Pacific-Wide Tsunami

The tsunami was triggered by a magnitude 8.8 earthquake on July 29 in the Kuril-Kamchatka subduction zone, where one tectonic plate is forced beneath another. The quake ranks as the sixth largest earthquake recorded anywhere in the world since 1900.

To study the event, researchers combined observations from the SWOT satellite with measurements from DART (Deep-ocean Assessment and Reporting of Tsunamis) buoys positioned throughout the Pacific. These instruments are designed to detect subtle changes in sea level and provide early warning information during tsunami events.

According to lead author Angel Ruiz-Angulo of the University of Iceland, the satellite provided a dramatically different perspective than scientists have had in the past.

"I think of SWOT data as a new pair of glasses," said Ruiz-Angulo. "Before, with DARTs we could only see the tsunami at specific points in the vastness of the ocean. There have been other satellites before, but they only see a thin line across a tsunami in the best-case scenario. Now, with SWOT, we can capture a swath up to about 120 kilometers wide, with unprecedented high-resolution data of the sea surface."

A Satellite Built to Study Water

SWOT was launched in December 2022 as a joint mission between NASA and the French space agency Centre National d'Etudes Spatiales. Its primary goal is to create the first comprehensive global survey of Earth's surface water, tracking everything from rivers and lakes to ocean features.

Ruiz-Angulo said he and co-author Charly de Marez had spent more than two years analyzing SWOT observations of ocean processes such as small eddies and currents before this rare opportunity appeared.

They "had been analyzing SWOT data for over two years understanding different processes in the ocean like small eddies, never imagining that we would be fortunate enough to capture a tsunami."

Challenging a Long-Held Assumption About Tsunamis

One of the study's most surprising findings involves a concept known as dispersion.

Scientists have traditionally viewed large tsunamis as "non-dispersive." Because their wavelengths are much longer than the depth of the ocean, researchers generally expect these waves to maintain a relatively consistent shape as they travel long distances.

In a dispersive wave system, however, different parts of the wave move at slightly different speeds. This can cause the original wave to spread out into a leading wave followed by a series of trailing waves.

"The SWOT data for this event has challenged the idea of big tsunamis being non-dispersive," Ruiz-Angulo explains.

When the team compared the observations with computer simulations, they found that models including dispersion matched the satellite measurements more closely than traditional tsunami models.

"The main impact that this observation has for tsunami modelers is that we are missing something in the models we used to run," Ruiz-Angulo added. "This 'extra' variability could represent that the main wave could be modulated by the trailing waves as it approaches some coast. We would need to quantify this excess of dispersive energy and evaluate if it has an impact that was not considered before."

Tsunami Data Reveals a Larger Earthquake Rupture

The tsunami observations also helped researchers refine their understanding of the earthquake itself.

Earlier models based on seismic measurements and land deformation predicted tsunami arrival times that did not fully match what was recorded by two DART gauges. One station detected the tsunami earlier than expected, while another recorded it later than predicted.

To investigate the discrepancy, the team used a technique called inversion, which works backward from observed tsunami behavior to estimate the characteristics of the earthquake that generated it.

Their analysis suggested the earthquake rupture extended farther south than previous studies indicated. The rupture stretched roughly 400 kilometers, significantly longer than the 300 kilometers estimated by earlier models.

Study co-author Diego Melgar noted that tsunami observations have become increasingly valuable for understanding how large earthquakes rupture near the seafloor.

"Ever since the 2011 magnitude 9.0 Tohoku-oki earthquake in Japan, we realized that the tsunami data had really valuable information for constraining shallow slip," said Melgar.

Why Multiple Data Sources Matter

Following the devastating 2011 Japan earthquake and tsunami, researchers began placing greater emphasis on combining different types of observations when studying major earthquakes.

Melgar explained that incorporating DART buoy measurements into earthquake analyses remains challenging because the physics used to model ocean waves differs from the physics used to model seismic waves traveling through Earth's crust.

Since then, Melgar's lab and others have been working on ways to include DART data in inversions, "but it is still not always done because the hydrodynamic models needed to model DARTs are very different than the seismic wave propagation ones for modeling the solid Earth data. But, as shown here again, it is really important we mix as many types of data as possible," Melgar said.

Improving Future Tsunami Warnings

The Kuril-Kamchatka subduction zone has produced some of the largest tsunamis ever recorded in the Pacific. A magnitude 9.0 earthquake in the same region triggered a massive tsunami in 1952, an event that ultimately helped drive the creation of the international tsunami warning system.

That warning network later played a key role in issuing Pacific-wide alerts during the 2025 tsunami.

As satellite technology continues to improve, researchers hope observations like those collected by SWOT could one day become part of near real time tsunami forecasting systems, helping provide faster and more accurate warnings for communities in harm's way.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough where a NASA satellite captured unprecedented detailed data of a major tsunami, revealing unexpected wave behavior and improving earthquake rupture models. This advancement has broad implications for enhancing tsunami forecasting and warning systems, which can save lives and protect coastal communities worldwide. The story is focused, detailed, and describes a real-world event with meaningful societal benefits.
articles.personality_title: NASA satellite reveals surprising behavior of giant Pacific tsunami
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – In July, a huge earthquake struck near Russia’s Kamchatka Peninsula, causing a massive tsunami to spread across the Pacific Ocean. This area is known for powerful earthquakes where one tectonic plate slides under another.

**What happened** – NASA’s new satellite called SWOT (Surface Water Ocean Topography) captured the first clear and wide view of this tsunami as it moved across the ocean. The satellite showed that the tsunami waves didn’t just move in a simple pattern but spread out and mixed in unexpected ways. Scientists combined this data with ocean sensors called DART buoys to study the tsunami’s size and timing.

**Impact** – This is the first time researchers have seen such detailed images of a large tsunami from space. The findings challenged old ideas about how tsunamis behave, showing that waves can spread and change shape during their journey. The data also revealed that the earthquake causing the tsunami was bigger than first thought, stretching further along the fault. This new knowledge can help improve tsunami warning systems and keep coastal communities safer.

**What’s next step** – Scientists plan to use SWOT and other tools to better understand how tsunamis form and travel. They hope to include this detailed satellite data in real-time warning systems to give people more accurate and faster alerts. This could help save lives by improving how we predict tsunami dangers in the future.

**One-sentence takeaway** – NASA’s SWOT satellite captured a giant Pacific tsunami in new detail, revealing surprising wave behavior and helping scientists improve earthquake and tsunami safety models.
articles.personality_title_fr: Un satellite de la NASA révèle un comportement surprenant d’un tsunami géant dans le Pacifique
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – En juillet, un énorme tremblement de terre a frappé près de la péninsule du Kamtchatka en Russie, provoquant un tsunami massif qui s’est propagé à travers l’océan Pacifique. Cette région est connue pour ses puissants tremblements de terre où une plaque tectonique glisse sous une autre.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Le nouveau satellite de la NASA appelé SWOT (Surface Water Ocean Topography) a capturé la première image claire et large de ce tsunami en mouvement. Le satellite a montré que les vagues du tsunami ne suivaient pas un simple motif, mais s’étalaient et se mélangeaient de façons inattendues. Les scientifiques ont combiné ces données avec des capteurs océaniques appelés bouées DART pour étudier la taille et le timing du tsunami.

**Impact** – C’est la première fois que les chercheurs voient des images aussi détaillées d’un grand tsunami depuis l’espace. Les résultats ont remis en question les anciennes idées sur le comportement des tsunamis, montrant que les vagues peuvent s’étendre et changer de forme pendant leur trajet. Les données ont aussi révélé que le tremblement de terre responsable du tsunami était plus grand que ce que l’on pensait, s’étendant sur une plus longue faille. Ces nouvelles connaissances peuvent aider à améliorer les systèmes d’alerte aux tsunamis et à protéger les communautés côtières.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques prévoient d’utiliser SWOT et d’autres outils pour mieux comprendre comment les tsunamis se forment et se déplacent. Ils espèrent intégrer ces données détaillées dans des systèmes d’alerte en temps réel pour fournir des avertissements plus précis et rapides. Cela pourrait sauver des vies en améliorant la prédiction des dangers liés aux tsunamis.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Le satellite SWOT de la NASA a capturé un tsunami géant du Pacifique avec un nouveau niveau de détail, révélant un comportement surprenant des vagues et aidant les scientifiques à améliorer les modèles de sécurité liés aux séismes et tsunamis.
articles.personality_title_es: Un satélite de la NASA revela un comportamiento sorprendente de un gran tsunami en el Pacífico
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – En julio, un gran terremoto ocurrió cerca de la península de Kamchatka en Rusia, causando un tsunami masivo que se extendió por el océano Pacífico. Esta zona es conocida por terremotos fuertes donde una placa tectónica se desliza debajo de otra.

**Qué pasó** – El nuevo satélite de la NASA llamado SWOT (Surface Water Ocean Topography) capturó la primera imagen clara y amplia de este tsunami en movimiento. El satélite mostró que las olas no se movían de forma simple, sino que se dispersaban y mezclaban de maneras inesperadas. Los científicos combinaron estos datos con sensores oceánicos llamados boyas DART para estudiar el tamaño y el tiempo del tsunami.

**Impacto** – Es la primera vez que los investigadores ven imágenes tan detalladas de un gran tsunami desde el espacio. Los hallazgos desafían las ideas antiguas sobre cómo se comportan los tsunamis, mostrando que las olas pueden expandirse y cambiar de forma durante su viaje. Los datos también revelaron que el terremoto que causó el tsunami fue más grande de lo que se pensaba, extendiéndose más a lo largo de la falla. Este nuevo conocimiento puede ayudar a mejorar los sistemas de alerta de tsunamis y proteger a las comunidades costeras.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos planean usar SWOT y otras herramientas para entender mejor cómo se forman y viajan los tsunamis. Esperan incluir estos datos detallados en sistemas de alerta en tiempo real para dar avisos más precisos y rápidos. Esto podría salvar vidas al mejorar la predicción de los peligros de tsunamis.

**Resumen en una frase** – El satélite SWOT de la NASA capturó un gran tsunami del Pacífico con nuevo nivel de detalle, revelando un comportamiento sorprendente de las olas y ayudando a mejorar los modelos de seguridad para terremotos y tsunamis.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed painting of a vast Pacific Ocean scene at dawn, featuring a stylized, gently undulating giant tsunami wave spreading in complex, interwoven patterns across the water’s surface, observed from above by a sleek, abstract satellite silhouette emitting soft beams of light, with distant subtle outlines of the Kamchatka Peninsula coastline under warm, natural hues of blue, teal, and soft orange.

articles.title: NASA at the Ion: Orion Lessons from Artemis II Shape NASA’s Moon to Mars Path
articles.summary: Seven weeks after the Orion spacecraft returned four astronauts from humanity’s first crewed journey around the Moon since Apollo, Artemis II Orion Vehicle Manager Branelle Rodriguez reflected on the mission’s achievements and how it is shaping NASA’s return to the lunar surface and future missions to Mars.  Introduced by NASA’s Johnson Space Center Acting Director of Business Development and Technology Integration Monte Goforth, Rodriguez spoke at the Ion in […]
articles.content:
Artemis II Orion Vehicle Manager Branelle Rodriguez speaks at the Ion in Houston on May 28, 2026.

Seven weeks after the Orion spacecraft returned four astronauts from humanity’s first crewed journey around the Moon since Apollo, Artemis II Orion Vehicle Manager Branelle Rodriguez reflected on the mission’s achievements and how it is shaping NASA’s return to the lunar surface and future missions to Mars.

Introduced by NASA’s Johnson Space Center Acting Director of Business Development and Technology Integration Monte Goforth, Rodriguez spoke at the Ion in Houston on May 28 as part of the NASA Stories at the Ion speaker series. Located in Houston’s Ion District, the innovation hub serves as a gathering place for entrepreneurs, researchers, and industry leaders working to advance technology and shape the future of industries ranging from aerospace to energy.

She shared an inside look at the mission she helped guide — as the Orion vehicle manager for Artemis II, Rodriguez has overseen the life of the spacecraft from end-to-end, through its development, production, execution of the mission, and currently, the post-mission work underway now that Orion is back at NASA’s Kennedy Space Center in Florida.

“This mission was very near and dear to my heart,” Rodriguez said. “It has not sunk in what this mission and what this accomplishment all means to us and humanity.”

From left: NASA’s Johnson Space Center Acting Director of Business Development and Technology Integration Monte Goforth, Artemis II Orion Vehicle Manager Branelle Rodriguez, and Director of the Rice Space Institute David Alexander.

Launched April 1, Artemis II carried NASA astronauts Reid Wiseman, Victor Glover, and Christina Koch, along with CSA (Canadian Space Agency) astronaut Jeremy Hansen, on a 10-day voyage around the Moon inside the Orion spacecraft.

Using mission imagery and video, Rodriguez walked attendees through key milestones, including launch aboard NASA’s SLS (Space Launch System) rocket, operations in high-Earth orbit, a lunar flyby, and Orion’s return to Earth. She also shared views from Orion captured by the crew, including Earthrise, detailed images of the lunar surface, and a solar eclipse observed from deep space.

Artemis II successfully demonstrated Orion’s performance during its first crewed deep space mission. The mission tested Orion’s life support systems, crew interfaces, navigation, and reentry systems, providing data that will help teams prepare for upcoming Artemis missions. The crew also completed a manual piloting demonstration, evaluating Orion handling and proximity operations that will inform future rendezvous and docking activities.

“I think it really hit me at T-minus 10 seconds,” Rodriguez said. “That’s when we go into ‘terminal count,’ meaning there’s just no turning back.”

Rodriguez emphasized that Orion’s success on Artemis II was the result of global teamwork across NASA centers, industry partners, and international agencies. She highlighted the European Service Module, provided by ESA (European Space Agency), which supplies Orion with power, propulsion, oxygen, water, and other resources needed during flight. In the Orion Mission Evaluation Room at Johnson, more than 300 people supported the mission, monitoring spacecraft systems and standing ready to respond in real time. Among the mission’s more personal touches was Rise, Orion’s zero-gravity indicator. The plushie, created by a student through an Artemis II design competition, carried a memory card containing over 5.6 million names of space fans who signed up through NASA’s “Send Your Name with Artemis” effort.

“It is what the crew wanted – to bring all of us with them on this mission,” Rodriguez said.

The crew also designed the mission patch with a hidden detail: viewed from a distance, the artwork reads “all” – a deliberate tribute to everyone who made the mission a success.

“It is a village that makes this possible, absolutely,” she said. Looking ahead, Rodriguez discussed preparations underway for upcoming Artemis missions. Artemis III will test critical rendezvous and docking capabilities between Orion and commercial human landing systems in low Earth orbit and advance plans to return astronauts to the lunar surface. On June 9, NASA announced the Artemis III crew at Johnson Space Center in Houston, while hardware for future missions is already in production at Kennedy Space Center in Florida.

For Rodriguez, Artemis II demonstrated what is possible when thousands of people work toward a common goal, supporting NASA’s vision of a sustained presence at the Moon and, ultimately, human missions to Mars.

“It’s going to take time to build this all up,” Rodriguez said. “But we are off and running.”
articles.reasoning: The article reports on the successful Artemis II mission, the first crewed journey around the Moon since Apollo, demonstrating key spacecraft systems and international collaboration. It highlights significant progress in human space exploration with broad inspirational impact and detailed context about the mission's achievements and future plans for lunar and Mars missions.
articles.personality_title: NASA’s Artemis II mission shows a path from the Moon to Mars
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – NASA recently completed Artemis II, the first crewed trip around the Moon since the Apollo missions. This mission tested the Orion spacecraft and prepared the way for future space exploration, including returning to the Moon and eventually traveling to Mars.

**What happened** – On April 1, 2026, four astronauts—three from NASA and one from the Canadian Space Agency—launched aboard the Orion spacecraft. They spent 10 days flying around the Moon before returning safely to Earth. Branelle Rodriguez, the Artemis II Orion Vehicle Manager, shared details about the mission at a Houston innovation center called the Ion on May 28. She explained how the mission tested important systems like life support, navigation, and manual piloting.

**Impact** – Artemis II proved that Orion can support humans on deep space missions. It showed how teamwork across NASA, international partners, and industry made the mission possible. For example, the spacecraft’s European Service Module, built by the European Space Agency, provided power and other essentials. The mission also included special touches like a plush toy carrying millions of names sent by space fans, helping people feel part of the journey.

**What's next step** – NASA is already preparing for Artemis III, which will test docking between Orion and new lunar landers. This mission will move closer to landing astronauts on the Moon again. Meanwhile, hardware for future missions is being built, and the Artemis III crew has been announced. These steps bring NASA closer to a long-term presence on the Moon and future trips to Mars.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Artemis II’s successful Moon mission tested key systems and teamwork that will help NASA return humans to the Moon and eventually send them to Mars.
articles.personality_title_fr: La mission Artemis II de la NASA ouvre la voie de la Lune à Mars
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La NASA a récemment mené à bien Artemis II, le premier voyage habité autour de la Lune depuis les missions Apollo. Cette mission a testé le vaisseau Orion et préparé le terrain pour de futures explorations spatiales, notamment un retour sur la Lune puis un voyage vers Mars.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Le 1er avril 2026, quatre astronautes – trois de la NASA et un de l’Agence spatiale canadienne – ont décollé à bord du vaisseau Orion. Ils ont passé 10 jours à orbiter autour de la Lune avant de revenir sur Terre en toute sécurité. Branelle Rodriguez, responsable du véhicule Orion pour Artemis II, a présenté les détails de la mission à Houston le 28 mai. Elle a expliqué comment la mission a testé des systèmes importants comme le support de vie, la navigation et le pilotage manuel.

**Impact** – Artemis II a prouvé qu’Orion peut soutenir des humains en mission dans l’espace lointain. Elle a montré comment la collaboration entre la NASA, ses partenaires internationaux et l’industrie a rendu la mission possible. Par exemple, le module de service européen, construit par l’Agence spatiale européenne, fournissait l’énergie et d’autres ressources essentielles. La mission comprenait aussi des détails symboliques, comme une peluche transportant des millions de noms envoyés par des fans de l’espace, pour les faire participer à l’aventure.

**Prochaine étape** – La NASA prépare déjà Artemis III, qui testera l’amarrage entre Orion et de nouveaux atterrisseurs lunaires. Cette mission rapprochera les astronautes d’un nouveau passage sur la Lune. Pendant ce temps, le matériel pour les missions futures est en cours de fabrication et l’équipage d’Artemis III a été annoncé. Ces avancées rapprochent la NASA d’une présence durable sur la Lune et de futurs voyages vers Mars.

**Résumé en une phrase** – La mission réussie Artemis II a testé des systèmes clés et la coopération qui aideront la NASA à ramener des humains sur la Lune et à les envoyer un jour sur Mars.
articles.personality_title_es: La misión Artemis II de la NASA marca el camino de la Luna a Marte
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – NASA completó recientemente Artemis II, el primer viaje tripulado alrededor de la Luna desde las misiones Apolo. Esta misión probó la nave Orion y preparó el camino para futuras exploraciones espaciales, incluyendo regresar a la Luna y eventualmente viajar a Marte.

**Qué pasó** – El 1 de abril de 2026, cuatro astronautas—tres de NASA y uno de la Agencia Espacial Canadiense—lanzaron a bordo de la nave Orion. Pasaron 10 días orbitando la Luna antes de regresar sanos y salvos a la Tierra. Branelle Rodriguez, gerente del vehículo Orion para Artemis II, compartió detalles de la misión en el centro de innovación Ion en Houston el 28 de mayo. Explicó cómo la misión probó sistemas importantes como soporte vital, navegación y pilotaje manual.

**Impacto** – Artemis II demostró que Orion puede apoyar a humanos en misiones en el espacio profundo. Mostró cómo el trabajo en equipo entre NASA, socios internacionales e industria hizo posible la misión. Por ejemplo, el Módulo de Servicio Europeo, construido por la Agencia Espacial Europea, proveyó energía y otros recursos esenciales. La misión también tuvo detalles especiales, como un peluche que llevaba millones de nombres enviados por fanáticos del espacio, para hacerlos parte del viaje.

**Próximo paso** – NASA ya se prepara para Artemis III, que probará el acoplamiento entre Orion y nuevos aterrizadores lunares. Esta misión acercará a los astronautas a un nuevo alunizaje. Mientras tanto, el equipo para futuras misiones se está construyendo y la tripulación de Artemis III ha sido anunciada. Estos pasos acercan a NASA a una presencia continua en la Luna y futuros viajes a Marte.

**Conclusión en una frase** – La exitosa misión Artemis II probó sistemas clave y trabajo en equipo que ayudarán a NASA a regresar humanos a la Luna y enviarlos a Marte.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a sleek spacecraft silhouette orbiting a softly glowing crescent Moon above a calm Earth seen from space, with a small plush toy floating nearby inside the spacecraft’s transparent window, symbolizing human connection and teamwork behind the Artemis II mission.

articles.title: Possible signs of ancient life on Mars are rich in complex carbon
articles.summary: An instrument on the Perseverance rover has identified large, complex carbon compounds alongside unusual patterns on the surface of rocks that resemble traces of microbial activity
articles.content:
An instrument on the Perseverance rover has identified large, complex carbon compounds alongside unusual patterns on the surface of rocks that resemble traces of microbial activity

NASA’s Perseverance Mars rover alongside a rock with markings that resemble features made by microbes NASA/JPL-Caltech/MSSS

NASA’s Perseverance rover has found complex carbon compounds in a Martian crater that had previously shown tantalising possible signs of ancient life. On Earth, these compounds are typically associated with dead organisms, but scientists say it is too early to draw the same conclusion here as these compounds are also found in lifeless environments, like on meteorites.

In 2024, Perseverance entered a rocky outcrop, called Bright Angel, near what appeared to be an ancient riverbed that once fed a lake inside Jezero crater. Several rocks photographed by the rover displayed unusual spotted patterns, which NASA scientists called “leopard spots” and “poppy seeds”. These markings, which are largely or entirely formed of dark, circular blots of up to a millimetre in size, look very similar to the patterns left behind by ancient microbial activity on Earth.

Advertisement

Although non-biological sources couldn’t be ruled out, the markings are some of the best candidates we have for potential ancient life on Mars. But scientists lacked comprehensive information on the chemical make-up of these patterns or how widely distributed they were in the Bright Angel formation.

However, Perseverance carries measurement tools that can provide more chemical context about the rocks it encounters, such as the SHERLOC instrument, which uses the reflected light from an ultraviolet laser to identify elements and compounds in a rock sample.

Now, Ashley Murphy at the Planetary Science Institute in Tucson, Arizona, and her colleagues have used SHERLOC to identify large, complex carbon-containing molecules, called macromolecular carbon, on the surface of the marked rocks in the Bright Angel formation, as well as in a separate rock in the same formation around 100 metres away.

Free newsletter Sign up to Launchpad Bring the galaxy to your inbox every month, with the latest space news, launches and astronomical occurrences from New Scientist’s Leah Crane. Sign up to newsletter

“On Earth, macromolecular carbon is often found in extremely old rocks and, in some cases, it is the only organic evidence of past microbial life,” says Murphy. “Finding these organic macromolecules on Mars and other planetary bodies helps us determine whether the necessary chemical ingredients and environmental conditions to support life have ever existed there.”

The existence of these carbon compounds can’t imply a biological origin by themselves, as they are also found in places like meteorites, says Lewis Dartnell at the University of Westminster in London. However, Murphy and her colleagues also discovered that the compounds were associated with carbonate and sulphate minerals, which tend to form in water-rich environments, another key ingredient for life. “It’s giving us information about the geological context of where those organics are being found,” says Dartnell.

Jezero crater was already suspected to have been water-rich at some point, so the fact that these carbon compounds existed here is unsurprising by itself, says team member Kyle Uckert at NASA’s Jet Propulsion Laboratory in California. However, macromolecular carbon has never been seen on the surface of a rock like this, says Uckert, which might imply that it is unusually resistant and different from other carbon-bearing compounds that have been found on Mars.

“Its ubiquitous presence within mudstones at Bright Angel compared with observations elsewhere in the crater was surprising,” says Uckert. Although it is currently unclear why that should be the case, this is a good sign for the possibility of finding other signs of past life, says Dartnell. “This detection confirms that complex organics, like these macromolecular deposits, can stick around for long periods of time.”

While the SHERLOC instrument can identify macromolecular carbon, it can’t give detailed information on the actual make-up of the compounds beyond saying that they are carbon-rich, says Sean McMahon at the University of Edinburgh, UK. “We would need to get the samples back to Earth to figure out if the carbon in these rocks was of biological origin,” he says.

Journal reference: Science Advances DOI: 10.1126/sciadv.adx0047
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery by NASA's Perseverance rover identifying complex carbon compounds on Mars that resemble signs of ancient microbial life. This finding has broad implications for understanding the possibility of past life beyond Earth, which is inspiring and emotionally uplifting for humanity. The story is focused, detailed, and highlights a major breakthrough in planetary science with potential large-scale impact on our knowledge of life in the universe.
articles.personality_title: NASA’s Perseverance rover finds complex carbon compounds on Mars that may hint at ancient life
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – NASA’s Perseverance rover is exploring Jezero crater on Mars, a place where water once existed. Scientists have been searching for signs that life may have existed on Mars long ago.

**What happened** – In 2024, Perseverance studied a rocky area called Bright Angel and found unusual spotted patterns on rocks. Using its SHERLOC instrument, the rover detected large, complex carbon molecules on these rocks. These molecules, called macromolecular carbon, are similar to those found in very old rocks on Earth that sometimes show past microbial life.

**Impact** – This discovery is important because it shows Mars has the chemical building blocks that support life, and the patterns on the rocks resemble those left by ancient microbes on Earth. The carbon compounds were found with minerals that form in water, reinforcing the idea that Mars once had conditions suitable for life. While the findings don’t prove life existed, they provide strong clues to study further.

**What’s next step** – Scientists hope to bring samples back to Earth for detailed study to confirm if the carbon compounds come from living organisms. Meanwhile, Perseverance will continue exploring and analyzing rocks to find more evidence about Mars’ past environment.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Perseverance’s discovery of complex carbon molecules and microbe-like patterns on Mars adds important clues about the planet’s potential to have supported ancient life.
articles.personality_title_fr: Le rover Perseverance de la NASA découvre des composés carbonés complexes sur Mars pouvant indiquer une vie ancienne
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le rover Perseverance de la NASA explore le cratère Jezero sur Mars, un lieu où de l’eau existait autrefois. Les scientifiques cherchent des signes qu’une vie a pu exister sur Mars il y a longtemps.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – En 2024, Perseverance a étudié une zone rocheuse appelée Bright Angel et a trouvé des motifs tachetés inhabituels sur des roches. Grâce à son instrument SHERLOC, le rover a détecté de grandes molécules complexes de carbone sur ces roches. Ces molécules, appelées carbone macromoléculaire, sont similaires à celles trouvées dans des roches très anciennes sur Terre, parfois liées à une vie microbienne passée.

**Impact** – Cette découverte est importante car elle montre que Mars possède les éléments chimiques nécessaires à la vie, et les motifs sur les roches ressemblent à ceux laissés par des microbes anciens sur Terre. Les composés carbonés ont été trouvés avec des minéraux formés en présence d’eau, renforçant l’idée que Mars avait des conditions favorables à la vie. Bien que cela ne prouve pas la vie, cela donne de fortes pistes pour continuer les recherches.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques espèrent ramener des échantillons sur Terre pour étudier en détail si ces composés carbonés proviennent d’organismes vivants. En attendant, Perseverance continuera d’explorer et d’analyser les roches pour trouver plus de preuves sur le passé de Mars.

**Résumé en une phrase** – La découverte par Perseverance de molécules carbonées complexes et de motifs ressemblant à des microbes sur Mars apporte des indices importants sur le potentiel de la planète à avoir abrité une vie ancienne.
articles.personality_title_es: El rover Perseverance de la NASA encuentra compuestos complejos de carbono en Marte que podrían indicar vida antigua
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El rover Perseverance de la NASA explora el cráter Jezero en Marte, un lugar donde hubo agua en el pasado. Los científicos buscan señales de que pudo haber vida en Marte hace mucho tiempo.

**Qué pasó** – En 2024, Perseverance estudió una zona rocosa llamada Bright Angel y encontró patrones manchados inusuales en las rocas. Usando su instrumento SHERLOC, el rover detectó moléculas grandes y complejas de carbono en estas rocas. Estas moléculas, llamadas carbono macromolecular, son similares a las que se encuentran en rocas muy antiguas en la Tierra, a veces relacionadas con vida microbiana antigua.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento es importante porque muestra que Marte tiene los ingredientes químicos necesarios para la vida, y los patrones en las rocas se parecen a los que dejan microbios antiguos en la Tierra. Los compuestos de carbono se encontraron junto a minerales que se forman en ambientes con agua, lo que refuerza la idea de que Marte tuvo condiciones para la vida. Aunque no prueba que hubo vida, ofrece pistas fuertes para seguir investigando.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos esperan traer muestras a la Tierra para estudiarlas en detalle y confirmar si los compuestos de carbono provienen de organismos vivos. Mientras tanto, Perseverance seguirá explorando y analizando rocas para encontrar más evidencia sobre el pasado de Marte.

**Resumen en una frase** – El descubrimiento de Perseverance de moléculas complejas de carbono y patrones similares a microbios en Marte aporta pistas importantes sobre el potencial del planeta para haber tenido vida antigua.
articles.image_prompt: A warm-toned, detailed painting of the Perseverance rover gently illuminating a rugged Martian rock surface marked with intricate, dark “leopard spot” patterns symbolizing ancient microbial traces, set within the dusty red landscape of Jezero crater under a soft, glowing sky, with subtle hints of shimmering carbonate and sulfate mineral textures embedded in the rocks.

articles.title: The lunar botanist with a plan to farm vegetables on the moon
articles.summary: Jessica Atkin knows more than anyone else about what it would take to supply food for a moon base. She reveals how to build a lunar farm and what astronauts can expect to dine on
articles.content:
Jessica Atkin knows more than anyone else about what it would take to supply food for a moon base. She reveals how to build a lunar farm and what astronauts can expect to dine on

KLAWE RZECZY

Astronaut Mark Watney is stranded on Mars and must somehow find a way to grow potatoes in the planet’s unforgiving, ochre soil before he starves. This is the plot of The Martian, which is very much a work of fiction. But for a real-life space botanist, look no further than Jessica Atkin.

Her skills might soon be in demand. After all, the recent Artemis II mission that circled the moon is a reminder that NASA is working towards a permanent base on the lunar surface. Sending pre-packaged food up from Earth may be enough to feed those living there for a while. But to make a moon base truly self-sustaining, its astronauts will need to grow their own crops.

Doing so is a tall order: in addition to colonists needing to mine the moon’s reserves of water ice to hydrate their crops, the harsh, volcanic lunar dust, or regolith, is remarkably hostile to plant life. That’s where Atkin, who is based at Texas A&M University, comes in. She recently showed that lunar regolith spiked with an alchemic mixture of organic matter and a special sort of fungus seems to allow chickpeas to sprout. The powers that be have taken notice: she has been awarded a large NASA grant to carry on with her research and crack how to grow veg on the moon.

New Scientist spoke to Atkin about setting up a laboratory in her own house, her dreams of lunar greenhouses – and what kind of meals future lunar astronauts can look forward to.

Robin George Andrews: Why did you choose to study space botany?

Free newsletter Sign up to Launchpad Bring the galaxy to your inbox every month, with the latest space news, launches and astronomical occurrences from New Scientist’s Leah Crane. Sign up to newsletter

Jessica Atkin: Some of my earliest memories are in a strawberry patch with my grandmother – and probably snacking on one or two along the way – so my connection to plants started quite early in childhood. Similarly, I’ve always loved space. I was raised by cowboys, and I would sit out on my tractor on the ranch every night, look at the moon and just think to myself: if we wanted to grow plants there, what would we do? On Earth, plants don’t grow alone. We need microbes. If they can help us colonise Earth, why can’t they help us colonise the moon? Nature gives us all the answers; we’ve just got to figure it all out.

But before entering academia, you served in the US armed forces.

The military was not the long-term plan. I knew if I was going to go to college, I wasn’t going to rely on my family to fund it. So, I said OK, what can I do? I knew if I put in my four years, the military could cover an undergraduate degree. I was a police officer. They sent me to Iraq to train the Iraqi police. When I came home, I was a firearms instructor. But I knew that lifestyle wasn’t for me. It’s not necessarily something I align with, but it did teach me a lot.

Why should we try to grow crops in lunar regolith, as opposed to shipping Earth soil up to the moon?

It could be up to $100,000 to send a pound [roughly half a kilogram] of any given material to the moon. So, we can’t really send soil. We are going to have to send prepacked meals, and we are going to have to use hydroponics, like they are on the International Space Station (ISS). But we should probably be investing in ways to do things to try to have a permanent presence.

Lunar regolith – the moon’s “soil” – is abysmal for agriculture, though, isn’t it?

There are so many issues with it. First, the structure is awful. The grains are small, sharp and positively charged – it sticks to everything, and it’s so sharp, it’ll cut micro-tears in plants. It even cuts micro-tears in astronauts’ suits. It’s a problem when you breathe it in. When you try to water something that’s like a baby powder, it forms like a cement, so it can’t trickle down to the plant roots – so everything suffocates.

Then there’s the chemical composition. It does have a lot of the things plants need: phosphorus, potassium, calcium, magnesium and trace micronutrients. Iron is also present and essential, but can be toxic to plants in high amounts. And there is a lot of aluminium, which is awful because it stunts plants’ growth.

The roots of chickpeas growing through simulated lunar dust in one of Atkin’s experiments Michael Miller/Texas A&M AgriLife

What have scientists done in the past to try to grow crops in lunar regolith?

There was a University of Florida team that showed you can grow thale cress in genuine lunar regolith brought back by the Apollo missions. It was stressed out, but it was amazing to show that plants can grow in real regolith. Organic material has been added to lunar-simulant soil, too. But when I started, microorganisms were largely ignored. Hold on, I thought, we’re missing a huge component.

And that’s where your work comes in…

I knew fungi were a key component of helping plants colonise land on Earth, so I wanted to explore whether that same partnership could help plants establish themselves in lunar regolith. If fungi helped plants build a foothold here on Earth, it made sense to see if they could help us do the same on the moon.

Why did you focus on chickpeas as a potential lunar crop?

They’re not your typical model crop. People grow lettuces, tomatoes – but legumes are largely ignored. They’re full of protein, and you’re going to need more than just lettuce if you’re going to keep everyone strong and healthy. Chickpeas also send out signals to recruit microorganisms to come work with them. They’re stress-tolerant; they don’t want to be watered that much. They don’t want to be bothered. Put them in the worst situation, and that’s where they do well.

Before you were awarded a prestigious NASA grant, you set up a plant lab in your own house, is that right?

It was in my living room!

Why did you need to set up a lab at home?

Back when I started this, in 2020, 2021, this kind of experiment wasn’t really what was done, and I wasn’t at a school where there were a lot of space people. Still, my professor at Texas A&M works on plant-microbe interactions and was willing to work with me on my idea. We wrote a proposal to NASA to try to get funding. The agency said the idea was great, but we didn’t have enough preliminary data. So, I bought all the supplies and did it myself.

Did you manage to use actual lunar regolith retrieved by the Apollo missions?

There is genuine regolith down at NASA’s Johnson Space Center in Houston, Texas, but they give that out very sparingly. I used simulant made from moon-like volcanic rocks found on Earth that replicates the upcoming Artemis landing sites, the lunar highlands. I gathered the preliminary data, wrote another grant to NASA, and that’s what was awarded.

Jessica Atkin managed to successfully grow chickpeas in simulated moon dust mixed with organic matter and fungi Michael Miller/Texas A&M AgriLife

Since then, you’ve moved into fully fledged laboratories to run your lunar chickpea experiments. How did they turn out?

We used fungi and different amounts of compost to see how much organic matter we need to get a healthy plant and healthy microbes. We found the mixtures of organic matter and fungi where the plants did best and formed the strongest symbiosis. It’s kind of wild that the chickpeas germinated quicker [than in terrestrial soil]. That said, any plant that was in regolith was stressed out; they do produce fewer seeds. I’m having the seeds tested at [Pennsylvania State University] right now to find out if they are toxic or not. I hope they’re not toxic, but even if they are, that’s not necessarily a bad thing because it means we’re biomining – the plants are taking these metals out of the regolith. For me, the point of this work isn’t the chickpea at all; it’s that we’re transforming this regolith into lunar soil, so we can put other plants in it.

Let’s say we have a moon base set up with a breathable atmosphere and filtered water. What challenges might we have in turning that into a lunar vegetable patch?

We’re going to have much more radiation than they’re used to on the ISS, and instead of microgravity, we’ll have one-sixth of Earth’s gravity. That could change how we water the plants. And we’re going to have to supplement lighting: we’ll have two weeks of lunar day, but then two weeks of lunar night. Also, astronauts don’t want regolith brought inside the habitat. They’ll want a separate greenhouse, so nobody’s ever exposed to the health hazards of lunar dust.

What about the protein-based parts of the human diet, like meat and fish?

Chickpeas have almost everything you need to replace that. I think that, for the foreseeable future, the protein-based part of an astronaut’s diet is going to be coming from shelf-stable, prepackaged foods.

At least they’ll be able to make space hummus, right? You just mix the ingredients in a blender.

There are not a lot of ways to cook in space. You need things to be eaten fresh. I always joke to my friends that I’m trying to open a falafel stand on the moon.

So, what’s the fanciest meal a lunar astronaut might feasibly have, grown from a lunar greenhouse?

Honestly, I would be going for sweet stuff, like fruits. I know they’ve been testing strawberries in space. NASA has been seeing how well strawberries grow in space-station-like lab environments on Earth, while strawberry seeds have been sent up to the ISS to examine how spaceflight affects their growth.

How do you feel about being called a lunar botanist?

It makes it hard to find a job. It’s so niche.

But if the Artemis programme proceeds as planned, surely NASA will need all the space botanists it can get, right?

That’s my hope! I’m just waiting for these positions to open. It’s so hard to create one-sixth gravity on Earth, or even in space. We need to get to the moon to actually test these things out. That’s my end goal. But I’m happy to contribute to the mission in any way I can, whether it’s on Earth or, hopefully, in space.

If NASA asked you to go to the moon to set up a lunar greenhouse, what would you say?

That’s the dream. I can tell them what size spacesuit I’d need – please just let me tag along. I joke that I’d be a moon janitor. I’d go up there and clean up the metals.

You’ve previously spoken about how your grandmother’s greenhouse helped spark your great interest in botany and agriculture when you were younger. Is she still around today?

She’s not – but she would be proud. She was always proud of me, for anything I did, as grandmas are. But she would just be tickled. She wouldn’t be surprised, though. I’ve always done the most random things.
articles.reasoning: The article describes a significant scientific advancement with broad implications for sustaining human life on the moon through lunar agriculture. Jessica Atkin's successful experiments growing chickpeas in simulated lunar regolith mixed with organic matter and fungi represent a tangible step toward establishing a self-sustaining lunar base, which has meaningful real-world impact for future space exploration and human colonization. The story is focused, detailed, and highlights a breakthrough with potential large-scale benefits for astronauts and space missions.
articles.personality_title: Scientists grow chickpeas in moon soil to help future lunar farms
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – NASA plans to build a permanent base on the moon, but astronauts will need to grow their own food to stay there long-term. The moon’s soil, called regolith, is very harsh and hard for plants to grow in.

**What happened** – Jessica Atkin, a space botanist at Texas A&M University, found a way to grow chickpeas in simulated moon soil mixed with organic matter and special fungi. Her experiments showed that adding fungi helps plants grow better in lunar dust, which is normally sharp and toxic to plants. Atkin started her research at home and later received a NASA grant to continue in a proper lab.

**Impact** – This research is important because sending food from Earth to the moon is very expensive and not practical for a long stay. Growing crops like chickpeas on the moon could provide astronauts with fresh, nutritious food and help turn lunar dust into usable soil. Chickpeas are a good choice because they are tough, protein-rich, and work well with helpful microbes.

**What's next step** – Atkin will keep testing how plants grow in moon soil mixtures and check if the plants are safe to eat. The goal is to create lunar greenhouses that protect astronauts from harmful dust and provide fresh food despite the moon’s difficult environment, including low gravity and strong radiation.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Growing chickpeas in moon soil with fungi is a key step toward making lunar farming possible for future space missions.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques cultivent des pois chiches dans le sol lunaire pour aider les fermes lunaires futures
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La NASA prévoit de construire une base permanente sur la lune, mais les astronautes devront cultiver leur propre nourriture pour y rester longtemps. Le sol lunaire, appelé régolithe, est très dur et difficile pour les plantes.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Jessica Atkin, botaniste spatiale à l’université Texas A&M, a réussi à faire pousser des pois chiches dans un sol lunaire simulé mélangé à de la matière organique et à des champignons spéciaux. Ses expériences montrent que les champignons aident les plantes à mieux pousser dans la poussière lunaire, qui est normalement tranchante et toxique pour les plantes. Atkin a commencé ses recherches chez elle avant d’obtenir une subvention de la NASA.

**Impact** – Cette recherche est importante car envoyer de la nourriture depuis la Terre vers la lune coûte très cher et n’est pas pratique pour un séjour long. Cultiver des cultures comme les pois chiches sur la lune pourrait fournir aux astronautes une nourriture fraîche et nutritive et aider à transformer la poussière lunaire en sol utilisable. Les pois chiches sont un bon choix car ils sont résistants, riches en protéines et collaborent bien avec les microbes.

**Prochaine étape** – Atkin va continuer à tester la croissance des plantes dans des mélanges de sol lunaire et vérifier si les plantes sont sûres à manger. L’objectif est de créer des serres lunaires qui protègent les astronautes de la poussière nocive et fournissent de la nourriture fraîche malgré l’environnement difficile de la lune, avec une faible gravité et de fortes radiations.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Cultiver des pois chiches dans le sol lunaire avec des champignons est une étape clé pour rendre possible l’agriculture lunaire lors des futures missions spatiales.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos cultivan garbanzos en suelo lunar para ayudar a futuras granjas en la luna
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La NASA planea construir una base permanente en la luna, pero los astronautas necesitarán cultivar su propia comida para quedarse allí a largo plazo. El suelo lunar, llamado regolito, es muy duro y difícil para las plantas.

**Qué pasó** – Jessica Atkin, botánica espacial en la Universidad Texas A&M, logró cultivar garbanzos en suelo lunar simulado mezclado con materia orgánica y hongos especiales. Sus experimentos mostraron que los hongos ayudan a las plantas a crecer mejor en el polvo lunar, que normalmente es afilado y tóxico para las plantas. Atkin comenzó su investigación en casa y luego recibió una beca de la NASA para continuar en un laboratorio.

**Impacto** – Esta investigación es importante porque enviar comida desde la Tierra a la luna es muy caro y no práctico para estancias largas. Cultivar cultivos como garbanzos en la luna podría proporcionar a los astronautas alimentos frescos y nutritivos y ayudar a convertir el polvo lunar en suelo útil. Los garbanzos son una buena opción porque son resistentes, ricos en proteínas y trabajan bien con microbios beneficiosos.

**Próximo paso** – Atkin seguirá probando cómo crecen las plantas en mezclas de suelo lunar y verificará si las plantas son seguras para comer. El objetivo es crear invernaderos lunares que protejan a los astronautas del polvo dañino y proporcionen comida fresca a pesar del ambiente difícil de la luna, con baja gravedad y alta radiación.

**Resumen en una frase** – Cultivar garbanzos en suelo lunar con hongos es un paso clave para hacer posible la agricultura lunar en futuras misiones espaciales.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a small, glowing lunar greenhouse nestled on the moon’s gray, textured regolith under a starry sky, with delicate chickpea plants sprouting vibrantly inside, their roots intertwined with glowing fungal threads symbolizing symbiosis, and soft Earthlight illuminating the scene with natural, muted tones.
